report_name: New 'GodDamn' Ransomware Disables Security Using Signed Microsoft Driver

threat_actors.name: Hyadina
threat_actors.description: A ransomware family and associated threat actor group responsible for the GodDamn, Beast, and Monster ransomware variants. This group utilizes advanced evasion techniques, including the use of signed malicious drivers (BYOVD) to disable security products.

attack_patterns.name: Remote Access Software
attack_patterns.description: Attackers deployed AnyDesk remote desktop software to establish persistent remote control over compromised systems.

attack_patterns.name: Kernel Modules and Extensions
attack_patterns.description: The attackers used a 'Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver' (BYOVD) technique by loading the legitimately signed 'PoisonX' kernel driver to execute malicious code with kernel-level privileges.

attack_patterns.name: Disable or Modify Tools
attack_patterns.description: The malicious 'PoisonX' kernel driver was used to programmatically terminate the processes and services of EDR and antivirus products to evade defenses.

attack_patterns.name: LSASS Memory
attack_patterns.description: The attackers used Mimikatz to perform credential dumping by accessing and extracting credentials from the LSASS process memory.

attack_patterns.name: Remote Services
attack_patterns.description: After stealing credentials, the attackers moved laterally to other systems on the network, likely using protocols such as RDP or SMB.

attack_patterns.name: Data Encrypted for Impact
attack_patterns.description: The final payload, GodDamn ransomware, was executed to encrypt files across the compromised network and accessible network shares.

malware.name: GodDamn
malware.description: A new ransomware variant belonging to the Hyadina family. It is distinguished by its use of the signed 'PoisonX' kernel driver to disable security software before encrypting files.

malware.name: Hyadina
malware.description: A ransomware family that includes the GodDamn, Beast, and Monster variants.

malware.name: PoisonX
malware.description: A malicious kernel driver possessing a valid Microsoft Windows Hardware Compatibility Publisher signature. It is used to terminate EDR and antivirus processes from the kernel level.

tools.name: AnyDesk
tools.description: Used by attackers to establish persistent remote access and command and control. The executable was observed hidden in a folder named 'Music'.

tools.name: Mimikatz
tools.description: Used for post-exploitation credential harvesting, specifically to dump credentials from LSASS memory after security tools were disabled.

tools.name: NirSoft
tools.description: A suite of utilities used by the attackers for discovery and information gathering, including stealing browser cookies.

identities.name: Microsoft
identities.description: The legitimate vendor whose Windows Hardware Compatibility Publisher signature was found on the malicious 'PoisonX' kernel driver, allowing it to be loaded without raising flags.

identities.name: Symantec
identities.description: A cybersecurity company that researched the GodDamn ransomware. The attackers also disguised the dropper for the PoisonX driver as a legitimate Symantec product.

infrastructure.name: GodDamn C2 Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Command and control infrastructure used by the attackers to maintain remote access via AnyDesk.

indicators.name: GodDamn Ransomware Associated Tooling Filenames
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: GodDamn Ransomware Associated Tooling Execution
id: 2b9c3d1a-8e7f-4b1c-9a0d-3e5f7b1a2c3d
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of tools and drivers associated with the GodDamn ransomware campaign, including AnyDesk, Mimikatz, and the PoisonX driver.
references:
  - https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/tag/malware/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-10
tags:
  - attack.t1219
  - attack.t1003.001
  - attack.t1547.006
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\anydesk.exe'
      - '\mimikatz.exe'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the presence or execution of files associated with the GodDamn ransomware attack chain.

indicators.name: Mimikatz LSASS Credential Dumping Command
indicators.pattern:
title: Mimikatz LSASS Credential Dumping Command
id: 8f9a0b1c-2d3e-4f5a-b6c7-d8e9f0a1b2c3
status: experimental
description: Detects the use of Mimikatz to dump credentials from LSASS, a technique used by GodDamn ransomware actors.
references:
  - https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/tag/malware/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-10
tags:
  - attack.t1003.001
level: critical
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\mimikatz.exe'
    CommandLine|contains: 'sekurlsa::logonpasswords'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects command-line patterns indicative of Mimikatz being used to dump credentials from LSASS memory.

indicators.name: Suspicious AnyDesk Execution from Non-Standard Path
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious AnyDesk Execution from Non-Standard Path
id: 4d5e6f7a-8b9c-4d1e-a2f3-a4b5c6d7e8f9
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of AnyDesk from unusual folder paths, such as a 'Music' folder, as observed in the GodDamn ransomware campaign.
references:
  - https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/tag/malware/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-10
tags:
  - attack.t1219
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\anydesk.exe'
    CurrentDirectory|contains:
      - '\Music'
      - '\Downloads'
      - '\Temp'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the execution of anydesk.exe from unusual locations, such as a 'Music' folder, which was a TTP in this campaign.

indicators.name: Potential Malicious Driver Service Installation (PoisonX)
indicators.pattern:
title: Potential Malicious Driver Service Installation (PoisonX)
id: 0b1c2d3e-4f5a-4b6c-8d9e-f0a1b2c3d4e5
status: experimental
description: 'Detects the installation of a new service, which could be related to the loading of the malicious PoisonX driver. This should be correlated with file creation events for new .sys files.'
references:
  - https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/tag/malware/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-07-10
tags:
  - attack.t1547.006
level: medium
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: system
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 7045
    ServiceName: 'PoisonX'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the installation of a new Windows service (Event ID 7045), which could indicate the loading of the malicious PoisonX driver.

relationships.source_name: Suspicious AnyDesk Execution from Non-Standard Path
